entry.title: iRPA - SAP Build Process Automation: cannot fetch automation job logs and output via Public API
entry.content:
Hi all,

I'm triggering an automation in SAP Build Process Automation through an API Trigger, using the endpoint:

POST /apiTriggers/{triggerId}/runs

This works correctly and returns a jobId. I would now like to fetch the logs and output stored against that job via the API.

The endpoint GET /apiTriggers/{triggerId}/runs/{jobId} only returns the job's status and timestamps — it does not include the log entries or the output values that are visible in Monitoring → Automation Jobs in the Build cockpit.

The API documentation isn't clear on how to retrieve this additional data. Could you confirm:

  1. Is it possible to fetch the logs of a job through the Public API, and if so, which endpoint should be used?
  2. Is it possible to fetch the output / result payload of a finished job, and via which endpoint?
  3. Are any specific scopes or role collections required beyond those used to trigger the run?

Any pointers to the relevant API documentation would be very helpful.

Thanks in advance.

Dinesh

entry.title: Re: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patc...
entry.content: Hi Andrea - You maybe right. if the dates show 2025, these may not be from current patch - maybe older files - as patch 5 is from Mar-Apr timeline. You may want to check the setupengine.log, specifically for last occurrence of deployfiles.bat - and see if the script eventually logged into the temporary CMS and there should be several uploaddfo success events - if thats the case, you are good to go. just move these files away from the packagestemp folder into some temp location. Anyway future updates come with their own files.

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: Q1-Q2 2026 Updates & Roadmap Highlights
entry.content:
Introduction

ESG reporting software is moving from an emerging category to a mission-critical business capability, driven by regulation, finance-led operating models, and a clear shift in buyer expectations. Even as regulatory scope evolves, demand remains strong: organizations still need to maintain or expand sustainability reporting, strengthen data integrity, and prepare for recurring disclosure requirements.

At the same time, manual and fragmented reporting models are no longer sustainable. Sustainability teams continue to face growing time burdens, computational complexity, and error risk when ESG data is collected across spreadsheets and disconnected processes. This is increasingly a finance-led concern as well: CFOs and finance leaders are becoming more influential in ESG funding decisions and are placing greater emphasis on the integrity of non-financial data.

AI is raising expectations even further. Business leaders increasingly expect reporting processes to become faster, more automated, and more intelligent. Still, sustainability AI can only create trusted business value when it is grounded in governed, traceable, enterprise-grade ESG data. Without this foundation, AI-assisted reporting can accelerate drafting, but it cannot reliably explain, evidence, or defend the data behind the disclosure.

This is where SAP Business AI becomes central to SAP Sustainability Control Tower. SAP’s approach is not AI added on top of disconnected sustainability data. It is AI embedded into business processes, supported by enterprise context, governed workflows, and traceable data from SAP and non-SAP systems. SAP Sustainability Control Tower is designed to support this journey by centralizing sustainability data and insights, helping organizations ensure compliant reporting, embed sustainability into core processes, and drive measurable progress across the enterprise.

These latest updates build on that foundation. They strengthen the data, integration, workflow, analytics, and reporting capabilities that make AI-assisted sustainability management credible in practice.

Here’s what’s new in SAP Sustainability Control Tower as of Q2 2026:

Expanded coverage of pre-delivered regulatory scope

To help organizations accelerate their sustainability reporting readiness, SAP Sustainability Control Tower continues to expand its SAP-provided ESG reporting content. These enhancements support customers by providing additional value for ESRS metric coverage across existing SAP-owned and custom metrics.

Recent master data enhancements include new and enhanced dimensions, measures, and data points. For dimensions, new and enhanced content includes gender attributes, human rights incident values, grievance mechanism recipient types, and severity of human rights incidents. For measures, new content includes human rights incidents, monetary sanctions due to human rights incidents, and enhanced naming for the count of governance body members.

SAP-provided metric coverage for ESRS data points has also been significantly expanded. As of April 2026, SAP Sustainability Control Tower includes 200+ quantitative metrics and 650+ qualitative metrics, supporting organizations with a broader set of content for ESRS data-point requirements. This includes quantitative coverage across areas such as climate change, pollution, water and marine resources, biodiversity and ecosystems, resource use and circular economy, own workforce, and business conduct. Qualitative coverage spans ESRS 2 general disclosures and topical standards across environmental, social, and governance topics. Documentation is available in Help for SAP Sustainability Control Tower.

That growing catalog gives customers a stronger starting point, while still leaving room to extend the model for company-specific or industry-specific reporting needs.

Data collection process enhancements

Governed ESG reporting depends on clear ownership, timing, and workflow. SAP Sustainability Control Tower supports form-based data collection for scoped measures and metrics, helping organizations define how data should be collected, when it should be collected, and who should provide and approve the submitted information.

With the data collection process, users can define data collection forms and set up data collection instances to trigger workflow-based data collection for selected items. They can assign processors for a given period and define approvers to review submitted data for approval or rejection.

Recent enhancements extend support to qualitative metrics in form-based data collection. Users can group qualitative metrics within a single form, and when new fiscal variants are activated, existing forms linked to inactive fiscal variants are automatically deactivated. In addition, table headers remain visible while scrolling, making larger data collection forms easier to use.

The qualitative metric data entry experience has also been redesigned. Users can select from all available metrics and add data at the high-level corporate summary level or at a more granular reporting structure level. Relevant details are available in the reference section on the data entry screen, helping users understand the context for the information they are entering.

The reporting line is now also available as an organizational boundary in data collection. Users can create a reporting scope using the reporting line organization type when setting up organizational boundaries, then run “check for updates” in the Configure Data Collection or Configure Data Entry app to add the new entries to the configurable items list.

SAP Sustainability Footprint Management integration

SAP Sustainability Control Tower also enhances integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management for greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions data. Customers can import GHG emissions data from SAP Sustainability Footprint Management into SAP Sustainability Control Tower while preserving native source granularity.

The integration helps eliminate the need for external data transformation and aggregation. Period type can be configured per integration scenario, with supported period types including monthly, quarterly, and yearly based on the active fiscal year variant. Data is ingested into SAP Sustainability Control Tower at the selected granularity, without forced transformation, and replicated to target systems using the configured period type.

Recent enhancements include a new UI control for period granularity selection and configurable period selection, including single-period and period-range options. Fiscal year variant support also helps enable more accurate period mapping.

The same principle of flexibility applies to the broader ESG data model, where customers often need to adapt standard content to their own structures and reporting practices.

ESG Data Foundation extensibility

As sustainability reporting requirements evolve, organizations need flexibility to adapt data models, extend content, and transport configuration across environments. SAP Sustainability Control Tower provides an open, extensible ESG Data Foundation that supports both standard SAP content and customer-specific extensions.

Customers can add custom measures across Data Provider Interfaces and add up to 20 custom dimensions for SAP-provided or custom measures. The content lifecycle for extension artifacts supports custom measures, dimensions, and metrics, with export and import via JSON file format.

Recent enhancements improve the content lifecycle for extension artifacts. Export and import are available in the Manage Metrics, Manage Measures, and Manage Custom Dimensions apps. Export selection can be based on naming conventions and includes dependent custom measures and custom dimensions to help ensure consistency.

Import supports consistency in the target tenant during object creation, and can now also update existing extensibility artifacts.

This makes extension management more practical across systems and tenants, especially as ESG reporting models continue to evolve.

SAP Datasphere content coverage

SAP Sustainability Control Tower also keeps enhancing its integration with SAP Datasphere. Users can leverage SAP Datasphere integration to load actual data into the Manage Quantitative ESG Data app and to upload master data for reporting structures, such as business locations, legal entities, and reporting lines, into the Manage Master Data app.

The SAP Datasphere content package supports multiple Data Provider Interfaces, including accounting, corruption incidents, emissions, generic measures, general measures, injury, land site, people, resource use, water withdrawal, waste, energy, and removal reversal carbon credit. Recent enhancements include updates for emissions and people DPIs, as well as new content for removal reversal and carbon credit scenarios.

The next step is to make those connected data flows easier to apply in regulation-specific scenarios, such as the EU Taxonomy.

EU Taxonomy data integration enhancements

For EU Taxonomy reporting, SAP Sustainability Control Tower now offers enhanced capabilities to manage master data and measures data in a more integrated way.

In the Manage Master Data app, users can configure and manage master data and EU Taxonomy filters in one place. This includes managing company code values used by EU Taxonomy in the legal entity dimension, defining filters applied to SAP S/4HANA master data and activity data, importing master data and hierarchies from files, and importing master data and hierarchies from SAP S/4HANA with the SAP Data Framework.

 

In the Manage Quantitative ESG Data app, users can import EU Taxonomy measures data, including activity data and denominator data. The integration supports imports from files and SAP S/4HANA, with validation rules to check consistency against master data and filters defined in the Manage Master Data app.

This simplifies how EU Taxonomy data is prepared, imported, validated, and exposed to downstream processes, while reducing the need for separate replication across the Manage Master Data app, the Manage Quantitative ESG Data app, and CPE-UM processes.

Analyze ESG Data app enhancements

SAP Sustainability Control Tower continues to evolve the Analyze ESG Data app from a reporting experience into a steering experience. The app helps users explore ESG performance with more flexible analysis, personalization, and target comparison capabilities.

Recent updates introduce target-versus-actual visualization in charts for selected metrics, periods, and reporting structures. This enables immediate performance comparison without additional configuration.

 

Users can also benefit from a flexible chart-specific configuration model that allows independent control over metric selection, filters, drilldowns, chart types, reusable public or private configurations, default views, and embedded descriptions for context.

The multi-chart analytics experience has also been enhanced. Users can display up to six charts in parallel, combine chart and table views, and adjust drilldowns directly in charts. This supports faster exploration and side-by-side analysis across different sustainability performance perspectives.

Generate ESG Reports with AI enhancements

The Generate ESG Reports with AI app matures as a way to reduce manual effort while giving users more control over ESG report creation. Users can generate ESG reports using prebuilt templates and AI-assisted drafting to support timely, accurate, and consistent reporting.

When generating a report, users can preview or download the result as a PDF. For added customization, users can manually edit sections, and reports are now available in DOCX format.

SAP’s built-in AI assistance supports dynamic revisions. Users can select a passage and use the Revise option to receive suggestions to shorten or lengthen the text, helping refine the final ESG narrative with greater ease and speed.

In addition, each report can be generated in one of 20 languages using AI-based translation. 

Planned features and roadmap highlights

Forward-looking statement: The following roadmap items are planned and may change.

Looking ahead to the remainder of 2026, SAP Sustainability Control Tower plans to continue strengthening regulatory coverage and embedded AI capabilities for audit-ready ESG reporting. 

Planned topics include further coverage of ESRS metrics, reporting scope management with inbound interfaces for materiality assessment results, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management integration for energy consumption, unit of measure management, evidence management for data collection, availability of sustainability metrics data product in SAP Business Data Cloud, integration with SAP Success Factors through SAP Business Data Cloud, validation rules, SOC 2 certification and user experience improvements for the overall reporting flow.

SAP Sustainability Control Tower also plans to expand support for IFRS sustainability disclosure requirements. Planned IFRS content provisioning includes comprehensive IFRS coverage, compatible metric definitions that enhance the ESRS offering, and IFRS-specific metric definitions.

Moreover, SAP Sustainability Control Tower is designed to support interoperability across sustainability reporting standards.  Within metric general information, customers can see interoperability between disclosure requirements across standards such as ESRS and IFRS, helping organizations address multiple reporting frameworks through a single reporting effort.

 

SAP also continues to explore AI-enabled support for sustainability regulatory readiness. The Sustainability Regulatory Readiness Agent is one of the planned agents, designed to help accelerate governed, audit-ready sustainability reporting by automating the assembly of reporting scope based on materiality assessments, creating data points for new regulations, mapping existing metrics, and proposing custom metrics where coverage gaps exist. 

Stay tuned for more and check SCT Roadmap Explorer.

Additional information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Control Tower, we recommend browsing the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

Stay tuned for more updates as SAP Sustainability Control Tower continues to help organizations move from ESG data to actionable insights, compliant reporting, and measurable sustainability performance.

entry.title: Re: Manage Maintenance Orders Cost Visibility
entry.content:
Hi MuratTnrkt,

 

As you mentioned, IW32 is displaying both planned and actual costs correctly, which indicates that PM order costing itself is functioning as expected.

The discrepancy appears to be specific to the Fiori app Manage Maintenance Orders (F5241), which derives and displays costs differently based on Ledger and G/L Account Hierarchy, resulting in a variation from what is seen in IW32.

I recommend checking with the FI/CO configuration team to review the following:

  • G/L account hierarchy configuration
  • Hierarchy assignment and default settings
  • Relevant cost element / G/L accounts
  • Analytical authorizations and CDS view access used by Fiori, to ensure there are no authorization or data visibility issues

Additionally, inactive ledger and currency fields are often default settings in S/4HANA, which can further influence how costs are presented in analytically driven Fiori apps.

Overall, this appears to be a Finance/Fiori configuration issue rather than a PM order costing issue.

For reference see the following SAP Community article for more information on managing hierarchies in SAP S/4HANA: Manage your hierarchies in FIORI 1/3 - SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Best Practices Recommendations for SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Hi Tomáš 

thank you so much for sharing your experience. What you say about designing spaces is exactly what I wanted to get across: "use as few spaces as possible to still effectively separate authorization - just as many as needed to do so."  If there is a use case for separating authorizations, like for securing HR topic or FI topic, a dedicated space should be set up, but the overall message should be: Try to keep the number of spaces small, to avoid an overhead of administrative tasks and shared entities. Of course, it is like with any authorization concept: Try to keep it simple, but allow the required limits and flexibility.

However, there is one further aspect that directs us towards a smaller number of spaces.  Within a space with many objects, you can group models into folders and sub-folders to separate topics and modelling layers. In contrast, if you create separate spaces, you need to share many objects, and these shared objects appear on the main level of the target space and can't easily be organized within the target space.

 

Regarding Virtualization versus Replication, it is really a matter of balancing the pro and con. Yes, Datasphere offers cheaper ways to store data sets, but so does BW if you use Near-line storage or Data Tiering Optimization. The main questions to decide this question are: 

* Do you need totally up-to date values (then go for virtual models) or is some latency acceptable (then replicate the data)?

* How often (and with which performance expectations) are the dataset analyzed repeatedly? (Replication is most important for frequently needed data.)

* How large is the replicated data volume? (Accessing large data sets across systems is rather slow, so the need to optimize performance is higher, but the storage will be more expensive, too.)

* Should we rather replicate values to reduce the stress on the source system? (Yes, try to extract only once instead of frequently accessing the data. But you don't want to replicate a data segment that is hardly ever needed.)

 

Persisting views (on final level before the Analytic Model) is a good idea to improve performance for a specific purpose, but it generates additional tasks or confusion when the view is changed. So, I recommend it mainly for short-term storage or for stable models.

I am looking forward to more and other opinions, experience, and discussion.

Thanks again for sharing your thoughts.

 Alexander

entry.title: Connecting SAP S/4HANA with SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh via Proxy Configuration
entry.content:
This blog post intends to complement the existing information on the following blog posts:

SAP S/4HANA integration with SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh

SAP S/4HANA and SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) direct integration

 

Whenever a proxy server is configured for SAP S/4HANA outbound traffic, certain configuration changes must be made, while other changes must be requested through a Service Request.

If your customer landscape uses a proxy server for outbound traffic that must be maintained in the SM59 destination, and you receive the following error when activating the AEM channel, this means that your RFC destination of type G does not support TCPS connectivity through a proxy server. In this case, the procedure for routing outbound traffic for your Event channels will be different. One possible solution is to implement a load balancer to route events from SAP S/4HANA, while using the proxy configuration only for the authentication service.

 

 

Requesting a Hyperscaler Load Balancer for Outbound Traffic

The TCPS protocol is required for the AMQP connectivity configured in the Event Channel. So, a solution is to establish this connection directly using a Hyperscaler Load Balancer. 

For that open a Service Request using the template Create Hyperscaler LB for OUTBOUND traffic to an EXTERNAL source(Azure/AWS/GCP).

 

Select the SAP S/4HANA system that needs to be connected with the AEM broker and insert the requested parameters:

Customer ID - the SAP S/4HANA system that needs to be connected.

Select Hyperscaler - the hyperscaler where the selected SAP S/4HANA system is hosted.

Primary or DR Site - if the service is to be performed on the Primary or the DR site.

Source IP Addresses - the IP address of the selected SAP S/4HANA system.

Destination IP Addresses - the Solace broker instance IP address or the host specified with protocol prefix: tcps://<hostname>

Destination Ports - 5671 (port used for AMQP connectivity)

 

Confirm and schedule service.

 

Updating the OAuth profile in SAP S/4HANA

You have created a AEM channel using a service key and the activation fails with the following error:

You have checked the OAuth configuration using the report OA2C_GENERIC_ACCESS, as described in KBA 3444844, and you are receiving the following error:

>> lcl_oa2c_app->EXECUTE_ATR
granttype Client Credentials configured.

HTTP failure, processing failed, invalid state, invalid timeout or others
oa2c_error
Client configuration error or network problems. See kernel traces.

 

Run transaction OA2C_CONFIG:

1. Select the Configuration Name created by your channel.

2. Under tab Administration - Access Settings - Add the values for proxy host and proxy port:

Save the OAuth2 Profile.

 

Run transaction SM59:

1. Select the type G destination created by your channel for the BTP AEM Validation Service.

2. Under Technical Settings - HTTP Proxy Options - Add the values for Proxy Host and Proxy Service:

Save and close.

 

3. Select the type G destination that you created for the Solace broker.

4. Under Technical Settings - Target System Settings - Maintain the same Host and Port used in Destination IP Addresses and Destination Ports requested in the SR Template Create Hyperscaler LB for OUTBOUND traffic to an EXTERNAL source(Azure/AWS/GCP).

 

5. Under Technical Settings - Target System Settings - HTTP Proxy Options - Remove the values for Proxy Host and Service:

Save and close.

 

Go to transaction  SE38 and execute the report OA2C_GENERIC_ACCESS again. Then confirm that you receive the following message:

 

 

Activating the AEM Channel in SAP S/4HANA

Run transaction IWXBE/CONFIG and activate your channel. Once the channel has been activated, perform a connectivity test.

If your channel activation fails, go to transaction SMICM and check the error in the Trace File.

entry.title: Entering the New Era of Agentic AI for ABAP Development
entry.content:
By Sonja Liénard, Head of ABAP Platform, SAP SE 

At SAP Sapphire 2026, we made a set of announcements that together mark the most significant step forward in ABAP AI since we first introduced SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities. Across tooling, agentic capabilities, platform reach, and commercial model, these announcements reflect a clear direction: making ABAP development smarter and more accessible to every customer — regardless of where they are in their SAP journey. 

Key highlights 

  • ABAP enters the era of agentic AI with the general availability of the ABAP MCP Server — open to your choice of agentic assistants, orchestrators, and LLMs  
  • ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code will be generally available in Q2 2026, completing our commitment to your choice of IDE for ABAP developers 
  • The first ABAP custom code agent for SAP S/4HANA migration releasing in Q2 2026 
  • ABAP AI available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, all releases from 2021, in Q2 2026 
  • A new consumption-based commercial model for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities — transparent, predictable, and well-suited for agentic AI workloads 

 

ABAP Enters the Era of Agentic AI 

At SAP TechEd 2025, we announced our intention to bring agentic AI to ABAP development. At Sapphire 2026, we shared that this is becoming generally available in Q2 2026. 

The foundation for this is the ABAP Language Server and the ABAP MCP Server built on top of it. The ABAP Language Server provides the abstraction layer that makes ABAP development capabilities available independently of the IDE.  

The ABAP MCP Server builds on this foundation using Model Context Protocol (MCP) — an emerging open standard that allows AI agents to interact with external tools and systems in a structured and reliable way. This opens ABAP development to the full breadth of the agentic AI ecosystem: coding agents such as GitHub Copilot or Amazon Q can now perform complex ABAP coding tasks directly within the developer's IDE. The MCP Server is available in both Eclipse and VS Code. 

The first ABAP agent built on this foundation — supporting custom code migration from ECC to SAP S/4HANA — becomes available in Q2 2026. 

An AI agent actively remediating ABAP findings in ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse in real time 

 

ABAP Development Tools for VS Code 

ABAP Development Tools (ADT) for Visual Studio Code (VS Code) will be generally available in Q2 2026. 

This is the living proof of our commitment to your IDE choice that we have been building toward for some time. VS Code is one of the world's most widely used IDEs, with a rich ecosystem of AI extensions. Building on the ABAP Language Server and ABAP MCP Server as the technical foundation, ADT for VS Code supports an agentic developer experience and gives ABAP developers direct access to modern coding agents like GitHub Copilot.  

The initial scope focuses on SAP Fiori app development, with additional object types to follow.  

Eclipse remains the IDE for full-fledged ABAP development until we reach feature parity with VS Code. Developers can choose between Eclipse and VS Code depending on their workflow and preference. 

Custom Code Agents for SAP S/4HANA Migration 

SAP S/4HANA migration projects carry significant complexity, and a large part of that complexity lies in custom ABAP code — business logic built up over many years, often undocumented and tightly coupled with SAP standard objects. Understanding it, adapting it, and validating it has historically been time consuming and expensive work.  

The Custom Code Assistant addresses this directly, bringing together a coherent, intelligent toolchain that covers all phases of an SAP S/4HANA custom code migration project. Three agents focusing on developer execution will be delivered by ABAP platform, complemented by additional agents from SAP Cloud ALM handling broader transformation governance. 

Custom Code Assistant with Custom Code Migration Agents 

The first CCM agent becomes available in Q2 2026 — the Mass S/4 Custom Code Conversion Agent, which identifies ATC issues for custom code adaptation and fixes them at scale, using deterministic rules or AI-generated fixes.  

Two further agents are planned for later in 2026: 

  • Mass Clean Core Adoption Agent — identifies clean-core ATC issues, fixes them, or provides structured recommendations where manual review is needed. 
  • Web Dynpro to SAP Fiori App Modernization Agent — converts legacy code to modern ABAP Cloud code. 

A core design principle across these agents is to first explain and understand the existing code before proposing any changes, to protect the investment customers have made in their custom code. Based on early evaluations, we are targeting 40% efficiency gain in custom code transformation work.  

Meeting You Where You Are 

At Sapphire, we announced that SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition for all releases from 2021 onwards, with general availability planned for Q2 2026. This removes a significant ABAP AI adoption barrier — customers on older releases gain the same AI-powered development experience as those on the latest release.  

This is made possible through a hub service running on SAP BTP, which combines authentication, license management, access to SAP BTP AI Core, and ABAP-specific business logic. The hub plugs directly into the developer's IDE, making it possible to offer ABAP AI capabilities largely independent of the ABAP backend release. 

A New Commercial Model  

ABAP AI capabilities are part of SAP Joule for Developers — SAP's AI offering designed specifically for the developer experience.  

As of Q2 2026, we are introducing a consumption-based pricing model for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities, built on AI units — transparent, predictable, and well-suited for the dynamic nature of agentic AI workloads. 

Activation is simple via SAP for Me, with users assigned through Cloud Identity Services. 

What This Means for ABAP Developers 

Taken together, these announcements tell a clear story: ABAP development is becoming more intelligent, more open, and more capable of tackling the hardest problems in large enterprise landscapes — at a pace and scale that was not possible before. 

The ABAP MCP Server opens ABAP to the full breadth of the AI agent ecosystem — your choice of orchestrators, LLMs, and IDEs. ADT for VS Code brings ABAP into the environment that developers already use. The Custom Code Migration Agents address the SAP S/4HANA migration challenge with purpose-built AI that understands the business logic and structure of enterprise systems. And the expanded platform support means these capabilities are available also to customers on older releases. 

This is about further development and collaboration — ABAP and developers remain a strong team. AI takes on more of the repetitive, time-consuming tasks, creating space for what creates real value: understanding the business, making good architectural decisions, and delivering solutions that last. 

Sonja Liénard is Head of ABAP Platform at SAP SE. She leads the unit responsible for ABAP platform, ABAP Cloud, ABAP AI, and ABAP IDE strategy.

entry.title: Re: Assigning fixed assets to functional location
entry.content: Assigning a fixed asset to a functional location is typically done in SAP Plant Maintenance (PM) / Asset Accounting (FI-AA) integration. There isn’t a direct “assignment” field in the asset master itself—instead, the link is maintained via the equipment master record, which connects both the asset and the functional location.

entry.title: Live session: SAP Datasphere - Replication Flow - Loading delta data from SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi friends of SAP Datasphere and data warehousing

you probably know hat SAP Datasphere does not just work like a data fabric, i. e. using the virtual approach to accessing data from various sources, but also incorporates the ideas of a Data Warehouse - loading data from multiple sources to a staging layer and further to higher layers of integration. 

take part tomorrow in the great journey of data from your sources to SAP Datasphere: Join the live session "SAP Datasphere - Replication Flow - Loading delta data from SAP S/4HANA"

https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions/sap-datasphere-replication-flow-loading-delta-data-from-sap-s-4hana?searchId=8a9a1ccd-c606-4e42-9e23-a92d9f0e04f0&listPosition=1 

We will demonstrate how to define a replication flow, and how to be efficient: instead of loading the same records again and again, you need only to load changed and new records. For this purpose the "Replication flow" of SAP datasphere can be implemented and scheduled in delta mode. Of course, it depends on the source object if and how this option is available. 

The next live class of this topic takes place 

May 13, 2026, 17:00 - 18:00 Europe/Berlin

and thereafter, on Aug. 12 and Sep 23

See you soon

 Alexander

entry.title: Re: Webi Formula for Aggregating amounts of Hierarchy

entry.title: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Argentina now in Roadmap for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Current Support Status

Electronic invoicing has been a regulatory requirement in Argentina since the early 2000s. SAP currently provides compliance support through different mechanisms depending on the product in use:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Electronic invoicing as part of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Argentina is already available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, providing support for header-level, detailed, and export invoicing scenarios.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise Editions

Electronic Invoicing for Argentina is now on the roadmap for these products. The planned solution will deliver end-to-end integration through SAP Document and Reporting Compliance cloud edition, connecting these S/4HANA editions with Argentina’s national tax authority.

Today these products are supported through ABAP transactions with open integration BAPIs. These ABAP transactions and their associated BAPIs are supported in accordance with the SAP S/4HANA and SAP ECC licensing models. Also, SAP NetWeaver Process Orchestration sample implementation instructions are available via SAP Notes to simplify tax office integration.

SAP NetWeaver Process Orchestration is approaching end of mainstream maintenance in the current year. In light of this, please be advised that effective 2027, the following will no longer be provided as part of the country localization solution:

  • Sample manual implementation instruction notes for SAP NetWeaver PO
  • SAP localization support for interface development or execution based on this integration layer

entry.title: Canvas report for employees active in 2025 (incl. terminated)
entry.content:
I need guidance on how to configure a Canvas report in SAP to extract all employees from a specific country who worked at any time during 2025. This must include active, terminated, and retired employees, as long as they were active for any period within the year.

Currently, I implemented a workaround using calculated fields:

  • One field to derive the previous year dynamically
  • A second field creating a flag based on status and termination year

However, this approach is not very maintainable.

Additionally, there is a data duplication issue: if an employee had multiple employment profiles during the year (e.g., Contingent Worker and later Employee), the query returns multiple records. The requirement is to return only one record per person, the most recent profile.

I would like to know if there is a simpler or standard approach to:

  1. Identify employees active during any portion of a given year
  2. Avoid duplicates and return only the latest employment record per person

entry.title: AI Product Management: Not Every Problem Deserves an Agent
entry.content:
Post 2 — AI Product Management series

The most expensive mistake in agentic AI does not happen in production. It happens before a single line of engineering is written, when a team decides to build an agent for a problem that should have been solved with a clearer dashboard, a better rule, or a redesigned human workflow.

The momentum behind "let's build an agent" is real. Executive sponsorship, vendor demos, competitive pressure. By the time a team reaches the architecture review, the decision to build has usually already been made implicitly. Discovery becomes a formality. The suitability question, should this be an agent at all, never gets asked at the right time, which is before the sprint starts.

This post is about that question and the framework for answering it.

The right comparison

The question is not "is the agent better than doing nothing?" It is "is the agent better than the current best alternative at comparable total cost?"

This distinction matters because agentic AI business cases almost always compare against the wrong baseline. The calculation usually looks like this: we spend X dollars per month on humans handling this workflow; an AI agent costs Y; Y is less than X; therefore the agent saves X minus Y. This calculation is wrong before it even gets to the cost model. The problem is the baseline itself.

The current best alternative for most enterprise workflows is not a blank page. It is a combination of experienced humans, rules-based automation, and tribal knowledge about which edge cases require escalation. That baseline has known costs and known failure modes. The correct comparison is the agent versus that baseline, including outsourced alternatives, not the agent versus nothing.

The four suitability conditions

Before any agentic project proceeds past discovery, four conditions must be evaluated. They are not a weighted scorecard. All four must pass. A single failing condition means the feature needs to be redesigned before engineering begins.

Condition 1: Repeats at volume. The task must occur frequently enough that the development, operational, and governance investment is justified by the throughput gain. An agent requires an evaluation pipeline, a monitoring stack, a governance process, and ongoing human oversight. These are not one-time build costs; they are recurring operational overhead. A task that happens twenty times a month rarely justifies that overhead. A task that happens ten thousand times a day usually does. The volume threshold is not a fixed number. It depends on task cost, the quality gap between human and agentic execution, and the cost of the governance infrastructure required. Calculate it before the sprint starts.

Condition 2: Outcome measurable and trusted. There must be a clear definition of what a correct output looks like, a reliable way to measure it, and organizational agreement that the measure is valid. This condition fails more often than teams expect. "Good customer service response" is not a measurable outcome. "Customer response rated 4 or higher by the recipient, with no escalation required within 48 hours" is measurable. If you cannot define done before you build, you cannot tell whether the agent is performing after you ship.

Condition 3: Tool use bounded. The set of systems and actions the agent needs access to must be enumerable and limited. An agent that "might need access to anything depending on the situation" has an authorization surface too large to govern. If the design requires unbounded tool access, the problem is not yet scoped well enough to be built safely.

Condition 4: Consequences recoverable. When the agent is wrong, the outcome must be reversible or correctable within an acceptable time window. Check this one first, before the other three. If the failure mode is irreversible, no amount of model quality or evaluation coverage makes autonomous operation safe. The correct response to a failed recovery condition is not "build better guardrails." It is a redesign toward the Copilot contract: the agent prepares the recommendation, and a human makes the final call.

An agent that autonomously approves vendor payments, deletes customer records, or executes financial transactions will fail condition four. That is not a reason to abandon the project. It is a reason to redesign it.

The context sufficiency check

Beyond the four conditions, the agent must have access to the context it needs to make correct decisions. Three categories of context routinely go missing in enterprise deployments.

Contractual and policy obligations. The customer whose data processing restriction exempts them from automated decisions. The vendor whose contract requires a named human approver for purchases above a specific amount. This context typically lives in legal or compliance systems not connected to the operational systems the agent reads.

Governance and relationship history. The account under active contract renegotiation. The vendor who failed a compliance audit six months ago. The customer whose last three tickets were escalated to legal. This context typically lives in email, in call notes, or in the institutional memory of the account manager, none of which are structured data.

Temporal validity. The status change that happened yesterday and has not yet propagated to the system of record the agent reads. The flag set based on data from two quarters ago that has not been refreshed. The agent sees the field. It cannot see when the field was last accurate.

If the context check reveals the agent is missing information in any of these categories, there are two correct responses: enrich the context before deployment, or design the agent to escalate to human judgment whenever the missing context would affect the decision. There is one incorrect response: assume the agent will figure it out.

The go/no-go memo

One page. Five questions. Required before any sprint starts.

Which of the four suitability conditions does this use case satisfy? If any fail, what is the redesigned scope?

What context does the agent need that it currently cannot see? What is the plan to close that gap before deployment?

Which of the Three Contracts does this feature operate under: Suggestion Engine, Copilot, or Autonomous Actor? Is that contract defensible given the consequence classification?

What is the realistic per-task cost at expected complexity? What does three-year total cost of ownership look like, including the governance stack?

If the agent is wrong at scale, what does recovery look like? Who is notified, how fast, and what is the compensating action?

If any question cannot be answered before engineering starts, the feature is not ready to be engineered.

 

When the answer is no

A go/no-go that produces "no" to autonomous operation is a correct product decision. Three paths forward.

Redesign to the Copilot contract. The agent prepares a decision package and presents it for human review. The human makes the final call. This passes the consequences-recoverable condition for almost any use case, delivers most of the operational value of the original proposal, and creates a safe foundation from which to evolve toward greater autonomy after the governance infrastructure is in place.

Defer with conditions. Identify the specific conditions that would change the assessment: context availability, volume threshold, governance infrastructure. Build toward those conditions explicitly, with a review date. This is not indefinite deferral. It is phased deployment with honest milestones.

Redesign the problem. Sometimes the agentic framing is wrong for the goal. The underlying problem may be better solved with a clearer dashboard, a more targeted rule, or a redesigned human workflow. The question is not "how do we make an agent work here?" It is "what is the best solution to this problem?"

A worked example

A product manager proposes an autonomous agent that reviews data retention change requests and approves or rejects them without human review. The proposal has clear volume justification and executive sponsorship.

Running the four conditions. Consequences recoverable: data deletion authorized by an incorrect agent decision cannot be undone. This condition fails immediately. Outcome measurable: the team cannot agree on what a correct decision looks like for edge cases involving conflicting contractual obligations. At risk. Context sufficiency — the agent cannot see the customer's contractual data processing obligations, active legal holds, or jurisdiction-specific retention requirements.

The correct design is the Copilot contract: the agent reviews the request, surfaces all relevant context it can find, flags cases where context is incomplete, and prepares a structured recommendation for a human reviewer who makes the final determination. This design passes all four conditions, delivers most of the value, and creates the context enrichment and governance infrastructure that would be needed before autonomous operation could ever be considered.

The project is not a failure because it did not reach autonomous operation. It is a success because it did not attempt autonomous operation before the conditions for it existed.

The next posts is The Cost Model Your Business Case Is Missing

The next post in this series takes the cost model apart: what the business case for agentic AI is almost certainly missing, why the estimates are systematically too low, and what a complete three-year total cost of ownership actually looks like.

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: AI Product Management: The Cost Model Your Business Case Is Missing
entry.content:
Post 3 — AI Product Management series

Business cases for agentic AI are systematically 40 to 60 percent too low. Not because people are careless. Because the cost structure of agentic AI is genuinely different from traditional software, and most of the frameworks PMs use to build business cases were designed for a different kind of system.

A note before we start: the model presented here is conceptual. It is designed to help product managers understand the fundamental cost dynamics of agentic AI, not to reflect the commercial terms of any specific vendor. It does not represent the pricing model for Joule, SAP BTP, or any SAP product. Customers may have specific agreements with SAP or AI vendors that materially change these numbers. Use this as a thinking framework, not a spreadsheet.

This post walks through what is missing, why the estimates are consistently wrong in the same direction, and what a complete cost model actually looks like.

The wrong baseline

The calculation almost always looks like this: we spend X dollars per month on humans handling this workflow; an AI agent costs Y; Y is less than X; therefore the agent saves X minus Y. This is wrong before it gets to any of the line items, because the baseline is wrong.

The correct comparison is not the agent versus onshore human labor. It is the agent versus the current best alternative at comparable total cost, which in most cases includes outsourced labor at $800 to $1,200 per agent per month, rules-based automation that is already partially handling the workflow, and a hybrid approach where AI handles simple cases and humans handle complex ones.

The business case that looks compelling at $28 per hour onshore often does not hold at $5 per hour outsourced. Most business cases never run that scenario. They should run it first.

How AI is actually priced

Most traditional SaaS has been priced per seat or per transaction at a flat rate. AI inference, by contrast, is usually priced per token. A token is roughly four characters of text; a typical sentence runs around 15 to 25 tokens. This matters because token pricing is not symmetric.

Input tokens (the text you send to the model, including instructions, context, and the user's request) are billed at one rate. Output tokens (the text the model generates in response) are typically charged at a higher rate, often two to six times more depending on the model and provider. Text generation is autoregressive: each token depends on the ones before it, which limits how much the provider can parallelize computation compared to one-shot input processing.

This shift from seats to consumption is already underway inside the SAP ecosystem. SAP Business Technology Platform uses a credit-based model where usage is measured in Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (CPEA) credits rather than pure named-user counts. SAP Business Data Cloud follows the same logic with Capacity Units (CU): you pay for compute, storage, and API calls consumed, not just for the right to log in. AI services running on BDC draw from that same CU pool. The implication is that an agentic workflow that runs frequently, retrieves large amounts of context, or generates long outputs will consume Capacity Units at a rate that is easy to underestimate at design time. The consumption model makes cost directly proportional to usage, which is the right structure for AI, but it also means there is no simple per-seat flat fee to anchor your estimate.

In current 2025 to 2026 pricing, the cost per task depends almost entirely on task complexity and model choice. Simple tasks such as email classification or FAQ responses often land in the range of $0.002 to $0.04 per task. Moderate tasks with two or three steps and occasional tool use tend to fall around $0.09 to $0.26. Complex tasks involving dispute resolution, five or more steps, large context windows, and extended reasoning can easily cost $0.50 to $2.00 or more. A business case built with simple-task pricing assumptions applied to a complex agentic workflow can underestimate real costs by an order of magnitude or more, sometimes by a factor of ten to fifty, depending on how aggressively the workflow uses context and generation.

The four cost surprises

Four mechanisms consistently produce cost overruns that teams did not see coming from their pricing estimates.

The first is input/output asymmetry. Any feature that generates long outputs (summaries, recommendations, detailed plans, drafted communications) is significantly more expensive per interaction than the input volume alone would suggest.

The second is reasoning depth. When a model works through a complex problem step by step before answering, the thinking process itself is billed as tokens. A model that reasons through a difficult escalation before drafting a response generates hundreds of extra tokens per call. At high volume, reasoning tokens become the dominant cost driver.

The third is RAG context accumulation. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) inserts relevant documents into the model's context before it responds. This improves quality, but every retrieved document adds input tokens. In a multi-step workflow, context accumulates: by step five, the agent's working memory includes the original input, all retrieved documents, and the outputs of every prior step. The cost at step five is much higher than the cost at step one.

The fourth is multimodal inputs. Any workflow that processes invoices, scanned forms, screenshots, or recorded calls carries a significantly higher per-interaction cost than a text-only workflow. This is routinely missed in estimates for document-heavy enterprise workflows.

The excluded costs

The software license is the smallest line item in a well-run agentic deployment. Research on RPA and traditional automation consistently finds that implementation and ongoing maintenance account for 70 to 75 percent of total three-year cost of ownership. The license is 25 to 30 percent.

Agentic AI adds a cost category that traditional software does not have: the observability and governance stack. This is the infrastructure for measuring whether the agent is performing correctly in production: evaluation pipelines, monitoring dashboards, drift detection, human escalation workflows, periodic audit processes. This is not a one-time build cost. It is permanent operational overhead.

Business cases that exclude the governance stack understate total cost of ownership by 30 to 50 percent. Gartner (February 2026) predicts that more than 40 percent of agentic AI projects will be canceled by 2027 due to unforeseen costs in this layer specifically.

The Klarna case

In February 2024, Klarna claimed its AI customer service deployment handled 2.3 million conversations in its first month, was equivalent to 700 full-time human customer support agents, and would generate $40 million in profit improvement for the year. This became one of the most cited proof points in enterprise AI business cases.

The $40 million figure was an estimate of cost avoidance from not hiring additional human agents to handle growth. It was not savings from replacing existing agents. Klarna's headcount reduction over the same period was attributed primarily to attrition and a hiring freeze, not AI-driven displacement. That distinction was absent from most coverage.

By May 2025, Klarna's CEO told Bloomberg that cost had been "too predominant an evaluation factor" and that the result was lower quality. Customer satisfaction dropped on complex interactions. The AI produced incorrect information in approximately 5 percent of conversations. Compliance concerns emerged around autonomous dispute handling. By September 2025, Klarna was redeploying engineers to customer support. By January 2026, the company was recruiting for remote human customer service roles.

The cost components excluded from the original $40M estimate: AI infrastructure operational cost, the observability and monitoring stack, customer satisfaction degradation on complex cases, customer retention impact, escalation premium when AI failed first, compliance risk and redesign, rehiring and retraining, institutional knowledge loss.

The reconstructed verdict: the $40M claim holds if the comparison is against hiring 700 additional onshore human customer support agents. It is substantially overstated against a hybrid deployment, and production data from 2026 shows hybrid deployments at 65 to 75 percent AI coverage closing the satisfaction gap to near-zero while retaining most of the cost reduction.

The SAP-specific wrinkle

For SAP customers, there is an extra variable in the cost equation. Many embedded Joule capabilities inside core SAP cloud products are bundled into existing subscriptions, so the incremental cost of light, in-app agentic features can appear close to zero because the underlying infrastructure is already paid for. For custom scenarios, higher volumes, or AI built on SAP AI Core and Business Technology Platform, however, usage still consumes metered AI or platform capacity, so the real marginal cost is not actually zero.

This cuts both ways. It makes the upfront business case easier to sell, because you can credibly start with "we already own most of the stack." It also creates a false sense of cost certainty, because the current pricing posture is explicitly temporary. SAP CEO Christian Klein has said publicly that SAP is shifting from per-user to AI consumption-based pricing as agents automate work that was previously done by licensed users, making seat-based revenue unsustainable. Build your business case on today's rates, but also run a stress test that asks whether it still holds if effective AI consumption costs significantly increase, say by two to three times, over the next few years. If the case only works at today's promotional economics, it is not robust enough.

The questions every business case should answer

Before presenting a cost model to your leadership team, three questions should have clear answers.

What is excluded? A business case that does not model the governance stack, the escalation premium, or the quality degradation on complex cases is not a cost model. It is an optimistic scenario.

Have you compared against outsourced humans, not just onshore ones? The scenario that matters most is often the one that is hardest to present, because it makes the business case harder to justify. Run it anyway. A project that cannot survive the right comparison should not be approved on the basis of the wrong one.

Have you included a repricing stress test? Current AI pricing reflects a land-grab phase. The vendors building the market today are absorbing costs to defend seat revenue. That phase ends. Model what happens when it does.

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: Re: SQL Anywhere on Linux
entry.content: What is the sizeof a SQLWCHAR in the unixODBC headers. The SQL Anywhere SQLWCHAR type is UTF16, not UTF32. There are Linux ODBC drivers that treat SQLWCHAR as 32-bit characters. Also, you could give the SQL Anywhere ODBC driver a try, compiling your application with the SQL Anywhere ODBC headers?

entry.title: AI across Customer Project processes
entry.content:
AI embedded in Cloud ERP systems helps simplify this process. Instead of working with disconnected tools and manual tasks, teams can manage the entire customer project lifecycle in one environment. AI supports decision-making, automates routine activities, and provides insights throughout the process—from opportunity creation to project monitoring and closure. 

That’s why AI across end-to-end processes drives better results for your business. 

But what does that look like in practice? Check out this video, which walks through how SAP helps teams manage customer projects from opportunity to delivery. 

What's on this page 

  1. From Lead to Opportunity in Seconds 
  2. Automated Project Setup 
  3. AI-Powered Staffing 
  4. Track Time smarter 
  5. Intelligent Billing & Profit Clarity 
  6. Performance Monitoring & Closure 

From Lead to Opportunity in Seconds 

The process starts with identifying and qualifying the right lead. The Deal Qualification Assistant enriches lead data, highlights buying signals, and keeps the pipeline updated in the background. 

Then, based on customer activity, the AI Assistant evaluates engagement and turns the lead into an opportunity within seconds. This reduces manual work for teams, while providing more insights and freeing up time for other tasks. 

Once the opportunity is created, Guided Selling Assistant recommends the next actions, flag missing information, and keep the deal on track. 

 After customer discussions, the quotation process begins. AI helps generate proposal communication and supports bid estimate preparation based on project requirements and historical project data. The estimate is then used to build the final proposal and move the deal toward closure. 

Automated Project Setup 

After the deal is confirmed, the project needs to be created in the system. 

With the help of The Project Setup agent, the project manager can create a new project within seconds. The manager simply prompts the agent to create a project and provides basic information of the Project. The agent automatically generates the project structure using predefined best practices. 

AI-Powered Staffing  

Once the project is created, the next step is to assign the right team members. 

The Advanced Skill Match capability analyzes employee skills, experience, and availability to identify the most suitable resources for the project. The project manager can review and compare suggested candidates and select the best fit for the required roles. 

This helps ensure that the project team is composed of specialists who match the project requirements.

Track Time smarter 

Accurate time and expense tracking are essential for project transparency. 

Instead of manually checking for missing entries, the Resource Manager can ask Joule to identify employees who have not recorded their working hours. Joule quickly returns a list of missing time entries and sends reminders to the relevant employees. 

This process takes only a few seconds, helping maintain healthy profitability and prevent revenue leakage. 

Intelligent Billing & Profit Clarity 

SAP Cloud ERP delivers precise, transparent billing across the entire project lifecycle, helping businesses protect project profitability and accelerate cash flow. 

During the pre-billing phase, teams can leave Smart Notes for clients and leads, providing full context behind each billed activity and reducing time spent on clarifications. 

From there, AI supports billing calculations based on rates, resource allocation, and workload, reducing manual effort and keeping the process faster and fully transparent. At the billing-closure stage, if any issues arise, Joule Assistant quickly resolves discrepancies and ensures accurate posting, so final invoices are sent without delays and payments arrive faster. 

Performance Monitoring & Closure 

Throughout the project, the manager monitors progress, financial status, and operational activities. 

AI-supported analytics in the Project Profitability Overview help track overall performance and provide actionable insights. Once all project activities are complete and results are validated, the manager wraps up the project efficiently, with transparent results and maximized profit.

entry.title: Licence Usage Report Demo for the Compensation Module
entry.content:
Introduction

Over the past few weeks I've been getting a fair amount of questions about the Licence Usage Report that was added to the Compensation Home screen in one of the 2025 release. Here is a quick visual explanation of how it works and how things are calculated on top of the existing documentation. 

 

Recording

In this 6 minutes recording we do a demonstration where we edit different compensation and variable pay templates to see how it impacts the Licence Usage Report.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

Archiving worksheets will not lead them to drop off the count. Only inactivating a template will but we don't recommend doing it unless there is a good reason for it (if your company downsized a lot then most likely the employees that are not with the company anymore are now Terminated in EC and fall off the licence count as a consequence as per example C in the first screenshot above).

 

Links

  • 3542507 - License Usage Report for Compensation Module

 

Thanks, 

Xavier

 

(If you found this blog useful please consider giving it a Kudo)

entry.title: Re: Create ALP with Chart and RAP V4 Service
entry.content:
Hi OsciPosci,

I came across this post looking for the exact same thing. After several days of investigating it against another RAP system, I also couldn't get an OData V4 ALP working. I'm posting this because your question has been unanswered for two months, and it's worth highlighting that this isn't an isolated case.

What I noticed: with another CDS (only with annotations @ui.chart, @ui.lineItem, @ui.selectionField, @Aggregation.default: #SUM), it works end-to-end with the FE V2 ALP template when exposed via OData V2 UI binding — table + chart + filter bar + KPIs, all native. The same CDS model, exposed via OData V4 UI binding, produces metadata that the ALP V4 wizard rejects with "must contain aggregate-based entities".

The issue seems to be in the combination of V4 binding and the V4 ALP wizard. What CAP emits and satisfies the wizard, the RAP V4 runtime doesn't emit in the same way.

If anyone from the community or SAP has managed to get ALP V4 working against RAP (not CAP) on S/4HANA on-premise, it would be really helpful to share a pointer or a sample. Also curious if there's a minimum Frontend Server / SAP_UI version where this is expected to work, since most of the documentation I've found is CAP-based. Any guidance is appreciated.

entry.title: You are the Voice of HR! Participate Now in Sapient Insights Group’s 29th Annual HR Systems Survey
entry.content:
The 29th Annual HR Systems Survey, conducted by Sapient Insights Group, is now open for participation through June 25th, 2025.  Your voice is the future of HR Technology!

 

TAKE THE SURVEY

This survey is valuable for anyone supporting their organization's HR systems and practices, interested in sharing their vendor experiences, and giving back to the HR community.

All responses are confidential and only used in aggregate results.

In 2025, over 4,600 organizations across the globe participated!

As a Thank You for your participation, you will receive the following:

  • Continuing education – Participants can earn up to 11 HRIP PDUs
    (Professional Development Units) - 10 for the survey + 1 more for the IHRIM member-exclusive webinar on December 16, 2026
  • A personalized data snapshot filtered by Industry or Region – your pick!
  • A full copy of the 29th Annual Sapient Insights 2026-2027 HR Systems Survey Report (350+ pages), an $1800 value, to be released in the Fall of 2026.

 

The first 100 respondents who complete the minimum survey requirements will also receive a $10 gift card of their choice or option to make a charitable donation, and our 29th, 129th, 529th, 1029th, and 2029th respondents will be awarded a $100 gift card of their choice or option to make a charitable donation.

The 29th Edition of the Annual HR Systems Survey includes these exciting updates:

The first 100 respondents who complete the minimum survey requirements will also receive a $10 gift card of their choice or option to make a $10 charitable donation, and our 29th, 129th, 529th, 1029th, and 2029th respondents will be awarded a $100 gift card or option to make a $100 charitable donation.

The 29th Edition of the Annual HR Systems Survey includes:

  • Extensive questions on the use of AI / Machine Learning throughout the survey
  • More options to collaborate with colleagues to complete the survey
  • A dynamic table of contents that allows you to easily navigate through the survey, making the best use of your time and expertise
  • Easily stored previous answers, so there is no pressure to complete the survey in one sitting
  • A 24-hour response time to any technical issues or survey questions
  • A YouTube video on “How to take the survey”

Survey topics include:

  • HR strategy, process, and structure
  • HR technology adoption in all applications
  • HR practices in Benefits, Talent Management, and Workforce planning
  • HRIS governance & organization structure
  • Functional & HR Tech support services
  • Deployment roadmaps, resourcing, and budgeting
  • Implementation, maintenance, and integration of HR Tech platforms
  • Change management and adaptive leadership practices
  • Emerging and innovative technology and trends

New section this year on finance, travel expense reporting, and financial planning

*The survey takes approximately 15-60+ minutes of your time and may be forwarded to other team members for additional feedback and knowledge sharing.

If you would like to preview the survey or if you have any additional questions before taking the survey, please contact Tammy Smith with the Sapient Insights Research team at research@sapientinsights.com.       

The survey results will debut at the HR Technology Conference October 20-22nd 2026. Our team looks forward to seeing you there! If you would like to purchase a copy of our 2025-2026 Annual HR Systems Report, you can do so here.

Thank you for sharing your voice and supporting this amazing global research community!

entry.title: How to define "PEER" Rater Category/Role in SuccessFactors 360 Reviews
entry.content:
I am new to SuccessFactors 360 Reviews, and I am learning to create a new 360 Review form with the workflow/process as mentioned below.

The form should be supported by a route map that allows the Employee to specify the participants. This selection will subsequently be reviewed and approved by the employee's manager, after which the evaluation will be forwarded to multiple raters, including Direct Reports, Peers, and/or the Manager. Upon completion of the evaluation, the confidentiality of the participants' feedback must be preserved from the employee, although it may be accessible to the manager and administrators. Attached are snapshots of the roles that are currently displayed under the form and the route map.

1.) How can I define the role category of a "peer," which could encompass anyone within the organization but outside the employee's direct hierarchy? For instance, this could include an EMM's counterpart or a colleague from the operations team, among others. Additionally, once the nomination of participants is approved, how does the Evaluation step incorporate these nominations to facilitate the feedback request?

2.) How are external participants defined ?

3.) How is such a role delineated within the Evaluation step of the route map?
